solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int findNumberOfLIS(vector<int>& nums) {
        int n = nums.size();
        if (n == 0) return 0;

        vector<int> lengths(n, 1);
        vector<int> counts(n, 1);

        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            for (int j = 0; j < i; ++j) {
                if (nums[j] < nums[i]) {
                    if (lengths[j] + 1 > lengths[i]) {
                        lengths[i] = lengths[j] + 1;
                        counts[i] = counts[j];
                    } else if (lengths[j] + 1 == lengths[i]) {
                        counts[i] += counts[j];
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        int longest = *max_element(lengths.begin(), lengths.end());
        int total = 0;

        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            if (lengths[i] == longest) {
                total += counts[i];
            }
        }

        return total;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n^2)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution uses dynamic programming to track the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at each index and the count of such subsequences. It iterates through the array, updating the length and count arrays based on previous values. The final result is the sum of counts for subsequences of maximum length. The approach is efficient for the given constraints.
